sections.header: Verses 1–34 — Families Who Resettled Jerusalem; Temple Servants

heb.paragraph: The chapter opens: “All Israel was listed in the genealogies recorded in the book of the kings of Israel and Judah. They were taken captive to Babylon because of their unfaithfulness” (v.1). The word for “unfaithfulness” is again maʿal — the Chronicler’s signature term. The genealogy now pivots to the PRESENT: who returned and resettled?

refs.note: Nehemiah’s list of Jerusalem’s residents closely parallels this chapter. Both draw on the same community records.
The list of returned exiles. Together with 1 Chr 9, these passages document the demographic foundation of Second Temple Judaism.

notes.note: The list of those who returned teaches that God preserves a remnant even through the worst catastrophe. Exile does not annihilate; it refines. The community that returns is smaller but purified. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The “book of the kings of Israel and Judah” may refer to the biblical book of Kings or to a larger court chronicle from which both Kings and Chronicles drew material. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

hist.context: The genealogies reach their destination: the post-exilic community. Judahites, Benjaminites, Ephramites, and Manassites who resettled Jerusalem are listed, followed by priestly families, Levites, gatekeepers, and temple servants. This chapter answers the question the entire genealogical section has been building toward: the returned community IS Israel. The people listed here are the direct heirs of Adam, Abraham, Jacob, Judah, and David. The unbroken genealogical chain from chapter 1 to chapter 9 proves continuity through catastrophe.

notes.note: These genealogical records are not mere ancient census data but the sovereign hand of God tracing His redemptive purposes through specific family lines. Every name preserved here testifies to God's faithfulness in maintaining the covenant lineage that would ultimately produce the Messiah (Matt 1:1-17; Luke 3:23-38).

The glory of the temple — its furnishings, priests, and sacrificial system — all point beyond themselves to the Person and work of Christ. The altar speaks of His cross; the veil of His flesh (Heb 10:20); the Holy of Holies of direct access to God through Him alone. No physical temple can contain the infinite God (Acts 7:48-50), but God graciously condescends to dwell among His people.

For the New Testament believer, the application is profound: we are now the temple of the Holy Spirit (1 Cor 6:19-20). The care, reverence, and holiness that characterized temple worship should characterize our daily lives. We are called to present our bodies as living sacrifices (Rom 12:1) — holy, acceptable to God, which is our reasonable service.

sections.header: Verses 35–44 — Saul’s Genealogy Repeated: Bridge to the Narrative

refs.note: The nearly identical genealogy in chapter 8. Minor textual differences suggest the Chronicler copied from two slightly different versions of the same source.
Saul’s death follows immediately. The genealogy identifies the character; the narrative evaluates his reign.

notes.note: The genealogy repeated at the threshold of the narrative teaches that individuals do not emerge from nowhere. Saul’s story is shaped by his ancestry, his tribe, and his place in Israel’s genealogical structure. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The nearly verbatim repetition of 8:29–40 is the most extensive internal parallel in Chronicles. Comparison reveals minor textual variants that are significant for establishing the Chronicler’s scribal methods. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

hist.context: The Saulide genealogy from 8:29–40 is repeated almost verbatim. The repetition creates an inclusio — a literary frame that signals the end of the genealogical section and the beginning of the narrative. The last genealogy before the story begins is Saul’s, because Saul’s death (chapter 10) is the event that brings David to the throne. The Chronicler’s story of Israel begins not with creation (already covered in the genealogies) but with the failure of the wrong king and the rise of the right one.

heb.paragraph: The Hebrew mālaḵ (to reign) carries the weight of divinely delegated authority. In the Chronicler's theology, every king reigns as God's representative — their faithfulness or failure directly affects the nation's destiny. The verb appears at the critical transitions that shape Israel's monarchic history.

notes.note: These genealogical records are not mere ancient census data but the sovereign hand of God tracing His redemptive purposes through specific family lines. Every name preserved here testifies to God's faithfulness in maintaining the covenant lineage that would ultimately produce the Messiah (Matt 1:1-17; Luke 3:23-38).

God's meticulous preservation of these genealogical records demonstrates His commitment to His covenantal promises. Each generation listed is a link in the chain of redemptive history, connecting the patriarchal promises to their ultimate fulfillment in Christ. God does not forget His Word, even across centuries of human failure.

Believers today are grafted into this same covenant lineage through faith in Christ (Gal 3:29). The God who faithfully preserved His people through these generations is the same God who preserves His church today. No purpose of God can be thwarted (Job 42:2), and no believer truly in Christ will be lost (John 10:28-29).

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVThe people from Benjamin, as listed in their genealogy, numbered 956. All these men were heads of their families.ESVand their kinsmen according to their generations, 956. All these were heads of fathers\u2019 houses according to their fathers\u2019 houses.NIVAll Israel was listed in the genealogies recorded in the book of the kings of Israel and Judah. They were taken captive to Babylon because of their unfaithfulness.ESVSo all Israel was recorded in genealogies, and these are written in the Book of the Kings of Israel. And Judah was taken into exile in Babylon because of their breach of faith.
